comment_text.sn16.11:1.2: For the Southern Hills or “Deccan” see note on SN 7.11:1.2.
comment_text.sn16.11:2.3: This is in reference to an event told in Kd 17:3.13.5.1, which refers to the rule on eating in groups at Bu Pc 32. Devadatta cultivated the support of then-Prince Ajātasattu and enjoyed lavish offerings. But with the failure of his attempts to assassinate the Buddha, his reputation disintegrated, so he and his cronies resorted to bugging families for invites for nice meals. The Buddha then banned eating in groups of four or more, except in certain cases. One of the exceptions is while traveling, which would seem to apply in this case. However, Kassapa is not exactly accusing them of breaking the rule, but reminding them of its purpose.
comment_text.sn16.11:3.2: These reasons are stated at Kd 17:3.13.18 and Pvr 7:33.12. | Devadatta was aiming to create a schism in the Saṅgha, which requires a quorum of at least four mendicants. To succeed, a schism requires not just the Saṅgha, but enough lay support as well. This rule prevents Saṅgha conspiring with lay people.
comment_text.sn16.11:4.5: Said of a monk sleeping in the Saṅgha (Thag 2.40:2.3) and of Māra (MN 50:21.4).
comment_text.sn16.11:5.1: Like the previous Sutta, these events probably took place shortly after the Buddha’s passing. The episode with Devadatta occurred only a few years before the Buddha passed away. Ānanda, though traditionally held to be the same age as the Buddha, must have been a generation younger. Here he’s probably around fifty or so, his hair greying, but still young compared with the grizzled ascetic Kassapa.
comment_text.sn16.11:7.2: Not only do Thullanandā and Thullatissā mistakenly think Ānanda was Videhan, they think Kassapa was a convert. Perhaps they are confusing him with fellow brahmins Sāriputta and Moggallāna, who formerly followed the wanderer Sañjaya (Kd 1:23.1.1). As so often, bigotry is the handmaiden of falsehood.
comment_text.sn16.11:8.7: He renounced independently, not following any school or teacher, much like the Buddha had. Another example is Pukkusāti (MN 140).
comment_text.sn16.11:8.8: MS edition reads the causative kāretvā (“had it made”), but surely Kassapa would have sewn his own robe, so prefer the variant kāritvā found in PTS, BJT, and Thai editions.
comment_text.sn16.11:9.1: There was another shrine of the same name near Vesālī (DN 16:3.46.3). They were, obviously, fertility shrines.
comment_text.sn16.11:12.2: For meditation “full of pleasure” (sātasahagata) see AN 8.63 and MN 128:31.4.
comment_text.sn16.11:13.2: Said by Bakkula at MN 124:38.1. See SN 6.1:8.2 and note.
comment_text.sn16.11:14.1: The sequence of events is unclear in the text.
comment_text.sn16.11:15.2: This singular honor took on a mythic significance, for which see Thag 18.1:11.4 and note.

root_text.sn16.11:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā mahākassapo rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn16.11:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā ānando dakkhiṇagirismiṁ cārikaṁ carati mahatā bhikkhusaṅghena saddhiṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:2.1: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmato ānandassa tiṁsamattā saddhivihārino bhikkhū sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattā bhavanti yebhuyyena kumārabhūtā.
root_text.sn16.11:2.2: Atha kho āyasmā ānando dakkhiṇagirismiṁ yathābhirantaṁ cārikaṁ caritvā yena rājagahaṁ veḷuvanaṁ kalandakanivāpo yenāyasmā mahākassapo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ mahākassapaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho āyasmantaṁ ānandaṁ āyasmā mahākassapo etadavoca:
root_text.sn16.11:2.3: “kati nu kho, āvuso ānanda, atthavase paṭicca bhagavatā kulesu tikabhojanaṁ paññattan”ti?
root_text.sn16.11:3.1: “Tayo kho, bhante kassapa, atthavase paṭicca bhagavatā kulesu tikabhojanaṁ paññattaṁ—
root_text.sn16.11:3.2: dummaṅkūnaṁ puggalānaṁ niggahāya pesalānaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ phāsuvihārāya, mā pāpicchā pakkhaṁ nissāya saṅghaṁ bhindeyyuṁ, kulānuddayatāya ca.
root_text.sn16.11:3.3: Ime kho, bhante kassapa, tayo atthavase paṭicca bhagavatā kulesu tikabhojanaṁ paññattan”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:4.1: “Atha kiñcarahi tvaṁ, āvuso ānanda, imehi navehi bhikkhūhi indriyesu aguttadvārehi bhojane amattaññūhi jāgariyaṁ ananuyuttehi saddhiṁ cārikaṁ carasi?
root_text.sn16.11:4.2: Sassaghātaṁ maññe carasi, kulūpaghātaṁ maññe carasi.
root_text.sn16.11:4.3: Olujjati kho te, āvuso ānanda, parisā;
root_text.sn16.11:5.1: “Api me, bhante kassapa, sirasmiṁ palitāni jātāni.
root_text.sn16.11:5.2: Atha ca pana mayaṁ ajjāpi āyasmato mahākassapassa kumārakavādā na muccāmā”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:5.3: “Tathā hi pana tvaṁ, āvuso ānanda, imehi navehi bhikkhūhi indriyesu aguttadvārehi bhojane amattaññūhi jāgariyaṁ ananuyuttehi saddhiṁ cārikaṁ carasi, sassaghātaṁ maññe carasi, kulūpaghātaṁ maññe carasi.
root_text.sn16.11:5.4: Olujjati kho te, āvuso ānanda, parisā;
root_text.sn16.11:6.2: “ayyena kira mahākassapena ayyo ānando vedehamuni kumārakavādena apasādito”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:7.1: Atha kho thullanandā bhikkhunī anattamanā anattamanavācaṁ nicchāresi:
root_text.sn16.11:7.2: “kiṁ pana ayyo mahākassapo aññatitthiyapubbo samāno ayyaṁ ānandaṁ vedehamuniṁ kumārakavādena apasādetabbaṁ maññatī”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:7.3: Assosi kho āyasmā mahākassapo thullanandāya bhikkhuniyā imaṁ vācaṁ bhāsamānāya.
root_text.sn16.11:8.1: Atha kho āyasmā mahākassapo āyasmantaṁ ānandaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn16.11:8.2: “tagghāvuso ānanda, thullanandāya bhikkhuniyā sahasā appaṭisaṅkhā vācā bhāsitā.
root_text.sn16.11:8.3: Yatvāhaṁ, āvuso, kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito, nābhijānāmi aññaṁ satthāraṁ uddisitā, aññatra tena bhagavatā arahatā sammāsambuddhena.
root_text.sn16.11:8.4: Pubbe me, āvuso, agārikabhūtassa sato etadahosi:
root_text.sn16.11:8.6: Nayidaṁ sukaraṁ agāraṁ ajjhāvasatā ekantaparipuṇṇaṁ ekantaparisuddhaṁ saṅkhalikhitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ carituṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:8.7: Yannūnāhaṁ kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajeyyan’ti.
root_text.sn16.11:8.8: So khvāhaṁ, āvuso, aparena samayena paṭapilotikānaṁ saṅghāṭiṁ kāretvā ye loke arahanto te uddissa kesamassuṁ ohāretvā kāsāyāni vatthāni acchādetvā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajiṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:9.1: So evaṁ pabbajito samāno addhānamaggappaṭipanno addasaṁ bhagavantaṁ antarā ca rājagahaṁ antarā ca nāḷandaṁ bahuputte cetiye nisinnaṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:9.7: So khvāhaṁ, āvuso, tattheva bhagavato pādesu sirasā nipatitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocaṁ:
root_text.sn16.11:9.10: Evaṁ vutte, maṁ, āvuso, bhagavā etadavoca:
root_text.sn16.11:9.11: ‘yo kho, kassapa, evaṁ sabbacetasā samannāgataṁ sāvakaṁ ajānaññeva vadeyya jānāmīti, apassaññeva vadeyya passāmīti, muddhāpi tassa vipateyya.
root_text.sn16.11:9.12: Ahaṁ kho pana, kassapa, jānaññeva vadāmi jānāmīti, passaññeva vadāmi passāmīti.
root_text.sn16.11:10.2: “tibbaṁ me hirottappaṁ paccupaṭṭhitaṁ bhavissati theresu navesu majjhimesū”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:11.2: “yaṁ kiñci dhammaṁ suṇissāmi kusalūpasaṁhitaṁ sabbaṁ taṁ aṭṭhiṁ katvā manasi karitvā sabbacetasā samannāharitvā ohitasoto dhammaṁ suṇissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:13.1: Atha kho maṁ, āvuso, bhagavā iminā ovādena ovaditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā pakkāmi.
root_text.sn16.11:13.2: Sattāhameva khvāhaṁ, āvuso, saraṇo raṭṭhapiṇḍaṁ bhuñjiṁ aṭṭhamiyā aññā udapādi.
root_text.sn16.11:14.1: Atha kho, āvuso, bhagavā maggā okkamma yena aññataraṁ rukkhamūlaṁ tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn16.11:14.2: Atha khvāhaṁ, āvuso, paṭapilotikānaṁ saṅghāṭiṁ catugguṇaṁ paññapetvā bhagavantaṁ etadavocaṁ:
root_text.sn16.11:14.3: ‘idha, bhante, bhagavā nisīdatu, yaṁ mamassa dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāyā’ti.
root_text.sn16.11:14.4: Nisīdi kho, āvuso, bhagavā paññatte āsane.
root_text.sn16.11:14.5: Nisajja kho maṁ, āvuso, bhagavā etadavoca:
root_text.sn16.11:14.7: ‘Paṭiggaṇhātu me, bhante, bhagavā paṭapilotikānaṁ saṅghāṭiṁ anukampaṁ upādāyā’ti.
root_text.sn16.11:14.8: ‘Dhāressasi pana me tvaṁ, kassapa, sāṇāni paṁsukūlāni nibbasanānī’ti.
root_text.sn16.11:14.10: So khvāhaṁ, āvuso, paṭapilotikānaṁ saṅghāṭiṁ bhagavato pādāsiṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:14.11: Ahaṁ pana bhagavato sāṇāni paṁsukūlāni nibbasanāni paṭipajjiṁ.
root_text.sn16.11:15.1: Yañhi taṁ, āvuso, sammā vadamāno vadeyya:
root_text.sn16.11:15.2: ‘bhagavato putto oraso mukhato jāto dhammajo dhammanimmito dhammadāyādo, paṭiggahitāni sāṇāni paṁsukūlāni nibbasanānī’ti, mamaṁ taṁ sammā vadamāno vadeyya:
root_text.sn16.11:15.3: ‘bhagavato putto oraso mukhato jāto dhammajo dhammanimmito dhammadāyādo, paṭiggahitāni sāṇāni paṁsukūlāni nibbasanānī’ti.
root_text.sn16.11:16.1: Ahaṁ kho, āvuso, yāvade ākaṅkhāmi vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn16.11:16.3: (navannaṁ anupubbavihārasamāpattīnaṁ pañcannañca abhiññānaṁ evaṁ vitthāro veditabbo.)
root_text.sn16.11:17.1: Ahaṁ kho, āvuso, āsavānaṁ khayā anāsavaṁ cetovimuttiṁ paññāvimuttiṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja viharāmi;
root_text.sn16.11:17.2: sattaratanaṁ vā, āvuso, nāgaṁ aḍḍhaṭṭhamaratanaṁ vā tālapattikāya chādetabbaṁ maññeyya, yo me cha abhiññā chādetabbaṁ maññeyyā”ti.
root_text.sn16.11:18.1: Cavittha ca pana thullanandā bhikkhunī brahmacariyamhāti.

translation_text.sn16.11:1.1: At one time Venerable Mahākassapa was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn16.11:1.2: Now at that time Venerable Ānanda was wandering in the Southern Hills together with a large Saṅgha of mendicants.
translation_text.sn16.11:2.1: And at that time thirty of Ānanda’s mendicant protégés resigned the training and returned to a lesser life. Most of them were youths.
translation_text.sn16.11:2.2: When Venerable Ānanda had wandered in the Southern Hills as long as he pleased, he set out for Rājagaha, to the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. He went up to Venerable Mahākassapa, bowed, and sat down to one side. Mahākassapa said to him:
translation_text.sn16.11:2.3: “Reverend Ānanda, for how many reasons did the Buddha lay down a rule against eating in groups of more than three among families?”
translation_text.sn16.11:3.1: “Sir, the Buddha laid down that rule for three reasons.
translation_text.sn16.11:3.2: For keeping difficult individuals in check and for the comfort of good-hearted mendicants. To prevent those of corrupt wishes from taking sides and dividing the Saṅgha. And out of consideration for families.
translation_text.sn16.11:3.3: These are the three reasons why the Buddha laid down that rule.”
translation_text.sn16.11:4.1: “So what then are you doing, wandering together with these junior mendicants? They don’t guard their sense doors, they eat too much, and they’re not committed to wakefulness.
translation_text.sn16.11:4.2: It’s like you’re wandering about wrecking crops and ruining families!
translation_text.sn16.11:4.3: Your following is falling away, Reverend Ānanda,
translation_text.sn16.11:4.4: and those just getting started are falling apart.
translation_text.sn16.11:4.5: Yet this boy knows no limit!”
translation_text.sn16.11:5.1: “Though there are grey hairs on my head,
translation_text.sn16.11:5.2: I still can’t escape being called a boy by Venerable Mahākassapa.”
translation_text.sn16.11:5.3: “It’s because you wander with these junior mendicants. …
translation_text.sn16.11:5.4: Your following is falling away, Reverend Ānanda,
translation_text.sn16.11:5.5: and those just getting started are falling apart.
translation_text.sn16.11:5.6: Yet this boy knows no limit!”
translation_text.sn16.11:6.1: The nun Thullanandā heard a rumor that
translation_text.sn16.11:6.2: the venerable Mahākassapa had rebuked the worthy Ānanda the Videhan sage by calling him a boy.
translation_text.sn16.11:7.1: She was upset and blurted out,
translation_text.sn16.11:7.2: “How can the venerable Mahākassapa, who formerly followed another religion, presume to rebuke the venerable Ānanda the Videhan sage by calling him a boy?”
translation_text.sn16.11:7.3: Mahākassapa heard Thullanandā say these words,
translation_text.sn16.11:8.2: “Indeed, Reverend Ānanda, the nun Thullanandā spoke rashly and without reflection.
translation_text.sn16.11:8.3: Since I shaved off my hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness, I don’t recall acknowledging any other teacher apart from the Blessed One, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha.
translation_text.sn16.11:8.4: Formerly when I was still a layman, I thought:
translation_text.sn16.11:8.5: ‘Life at home is cramped and dirty, life gone forth is wide open.
translation_text.sn16.11:8.6: It’s not easy for someone living at home to lead the spiritual life utterly full and pure, like a polished shell.
translation_text.sn16.11:8.7: Why don’t I shave off my hair and beard, dress in ocher robes, and go forth from the lay life to homelessness?’
translation_text.sn16.11:8.8: After some time I made an outer robe of patches and, in the name of the perfected ones in the world, I shaved off my hair and beard, dressed in ocher robes, and went forth from the lay life to homelessness.
translation_text.sn16.11:9.1: When I had gone forth, I traveled along the road between Rājagaha and Nāḷandā, where I saw the Buddha sitting at the Many Sons Shrine.
translation_text.sn16.11:9.3: ‘Were I ever to see a Teacher, it would be this Blessed One!
translation_text.sn16.11:9.4: Were I ever to see a Holy One, it would be this Blessed One!
translation_text.sn16.11:9.5: Were I ever to see a fully awakened Buddha, it would be this Blessed One!’
translation_text.sn16.11:9.7: Then I bowed with my head at the Buddha’s feet and said:
translation_text.sn16.11:9.8: ‘Sir, the Buddha is my Teacher, I am his disciple!
translation_text.sn16.11:9.9: The Buddha is my Teacher, I am his disciple!’
translation_text.sn16.11:9.11: ‘Kassapa, if anyone was to say to such a wholehearted disciple that they know when they don’t know, or that they see when they don’t see, their head would explode.
translation_text.sn16.11:9.12: But Kassapa, when I say that I know I really do know; and when I say that I see I really do see.
translation_text.sn16.11:10.1: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn16.11:10.2: “I will set up a keen sense of conscience and prudence for seniors, juniors, and those in the middle.”
translation_text.sn16.11:11.1: And you should train like this:
translation_text.sn16.11:11.2: “Whenever I hear a teaching connected with what’s skillful, I will pay attention, apply the mind, concentrate wholeheartedly, and actively listen to that teaching.”
translation_text.sn16.11:12.1: And you should train like this:
translation_text.sn16.11:12.2: “I will never neglect mindfulness of the body that is full of pleasure.”
translation_text.sn16.11:13.1: And when the Buddha had given me this advice he got up from his seat and left.
translation_text.sn16.11:13.2: For seven days I ate the nation’s almsfood as a debtor. On the eighth day I was enlightened.
translation_text.sn16.11:14.1: And then the Buddha left the road and went to the root of a certain tree.
translation_text.sn16.11:14.2: So I spread out my outer robe of patches folded in four and said to him,
translation_text.sn16.11:14.3: ‘Sir, sit here. That would be for my lasting welfare and happiness.’
translation_text.sn16.11:14.4: The Buddha sat on the seat spread out
translation_text.sn16.11:14.6: ‘Kassapa, this outer robe of patches is soft.’
translation_text.sn16.11:14.7: ‘Sir, please accept my outer robe of patches out of sympathy.’
translation_text.sn16.11:14.8: ‘In that case, Kassapa, will you wear my worn-out hempen rag robe?’
translation_text.sn16.11:14.10: And so I presented my outer robe of patches to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn16.11:14.11: and the Buddha presented me with his worn-out hempen rag robe.
translation_text.sn16.11:15.1: For if anyone should be rightly called
translation_text.sn16.11:15.2: the Buddha’s true-born son, born from his mouth, born of the teaching, created by the teaching, heir to the teaching, and receiver of his worn-out hempen rag robes, it’s me.
translation_text.sn16.11:16.1: Whenever I want, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter and remain in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected. …
translation_text.sn16.11:16.3: (The nine progressive meditations and the five insights should be told in full.)
translation_text.sn16.11:17.1: I have realized the undefiled freedom of heart and freedom by wisdom in this very life. And I live having realized it with my own insight due to the ending of defilements.
translation_text.sn16.11:17.2: Reverend, you might as well think to hide a bull elephant that’s three or three and a half meters tall behind a palm leaf as to hide my six insights.”
translation_text.sn16.11:18.1: But the nun Thullanandā fell from the spiritual life.

variant_text.sn16.11:5.5: palujjanti kho te, āvuso → palujjati kho te āvuso ānanda parisā (bj)
variant_text.sn16.11:8.8: kāretvā → karitvā (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
